title: Looping Arrays 🎢
description: The perfect combination of variables and repetition.

slides.title: Match Made in Heaven 💖
slides.content:
Arrays and `for` loops were built for each other.

An array holds items at indexes 0, 1, 2, 3, etc.
A `for(int i=0; ...)` loop naturally counts 0, 1, 2, 3.

We can use the `i` from the loop to peek into every single slot of an array!

slides.title: Printing the Backpack 🎒
slides.content: Every array has a special `.length` property that tells you exactly how many items are in it. This makes it incredibly easy to loop through perfectly without going "Out of Bounds".

quizData.question: In the code above, `backpack.length` equals 4. Why did we use `<` instead of `<=` in the `for` loop?

options.text: Because index 4 doesn't exist (it goes 0, 1, 2, 3), so checking <= 4 would throw an Out Of Bounds Error.
Because Java doesn't have a <= operator.

quizData.explanation: If we used `<=`, the loop would eventually ask for `backpack[4]`. But the final item is at index 3. It would crash!

slides.title: The Enhanced For Loop ✨
slides.content:
Because looping through an entire array one-by-one is so common, Java has a shortcut loop specifically for Arrays. It's called the **For-Each** loop.

It entirely hides the confusing `int i=0...` stuff and just gives you the item directly.

quizData.question: If you just want to print every item in an array and don't care about their specific Index addresses, which loop is visually cleaner?

options.text: The enhanced FOR-EACH loop (`for (Type t : array)`)

quizData.explanation: The Enhanced for-loop is designed to cleanly rip through every item without the manual index integer tracking.
